title: The Continuous-Time Joint Replenishment Problem:ϵ-Optimal Policies via Pairwise Alignment
abstract: The main contribution of this paper resides in developing a new algorithmic approach for addressing the continuous-time joint replenishment problem, termedΨ-pairwise alignment. The latter mechanism, through which we synchronize multiple economic order quantity models, allows us to devise a purely combinatorial algorithm for efficiently approximating optimal policies within any degree of accuracy. As a result, our work constitutes the first quantitative improvement over power-of-2 policies, which have been state-of-the-art in this context since the mid-1980s. Moreover, in light of recent intractability results, by proposing an efficient polynomial-time approximation scheme for the joint replenishment problem, we resolve the long-standing open question regarding the computational complexity of this classical setting.

author.Danny Segev: School of Mathematical Sciences and Coller School of Management, Tel Aviv University, Tel Aviv 69978, Israel

article.1. Introduction: Dating back to the mid-1960s, with indirect investigations surfacing even earlier, the joint replenishment problem has been playing an instrumental role in developing the theoretical foundations of inventory management, as well as in boosting the practical appeal of this academic field. Given the massive body of work along these lines, spanning rigorous methods, heuristics, experimental studies, real-life applications, and software solutions, we cannot do justice and present an exhaustive overview of this literature beyond directly related results. We refer avid readers to excellent survey articles (Aksoy and Erenguc 1988,Goyal and Satir 1989,Muckstadt and Roundy 1993,Khouja and Goyal 2008,Bastos et al. 2017) and book chapters (Silver and Peterson 1985,Zipkin 2000,Muckstadt and Sapra 2010), as well as to the references therein, for an in-depth discussion in this context. By consulting the above-mentioned resources, it is apparent that, although joint replenishment settings have been studied in various forms and shapes, they are all inherently concerned with the lot-sizing of multiple commodities over a given planning horizon. Here, our seemingly simple objective is to determine a joint replenishment policy that minimizes long-run average operating costs. However, these circumstances give rise to unique computational obstacles regarding the efficient synchronization of numerous economic order quantity (EOQ) models along with wide-open analytical questions regarding their structural characterization. In a nutshell, on top of commodity-specific ordering and inventory holding costs, what makes such synchronization particularly challenging is the interplay between different commodities via joint ordering costs, incurred whenever an order is placed, regardless of its contents. To take a deeper dive into these questions and to accurately position the main contributions of our work, we proceed by providing a formal mathematical description of the joint replenishment problem in its broadest continuous-time form. 1.1. Model Formulation 1.1.1. EOQ Model. For ease of exposition, it is convenient to start off by describing the basic building block of joint replenishment settings: the EOQ model. Here, we wish to determine the time intervalTbetween successive orders of a single commodity, aiming to minimize our long-run average cost over the continuous planning horizon[0,∞). Specifically, this commodity is associated with a stationary demand rate ofdto be fully satisfied upon occurrence, meaning that lost sales and back orders are not permitted. In this context, periodic policies are simply those where the ordering frequency is uniform, namely orders will be placed at time points0,T,2T,3T,…, where the time intervalTis a decision variable to be optimized. In turn, each of these orders incurs a fixed cost ofK, regardless of its quantity. This ingredient is complemented by a linear holding cost ofh, incurred per time unit for each inventory unit in stock. Our objective is to determine the time intervalTto minimize long-run average ordering and holding costs. Based on this description, one can easily verify that, in an optimal policy, orders will be placed only when the current on-hand inventory drops to zero, that is, zero inventory ordering (ZIO) policies are optimal. As such, the objective function of interest admits a succinct representation, given by The cost functionC:(0,∞)→R++satisfies the following properties: C is strictly convex. The unique minimizerof C isT*=K/H. C(θ·T*)=12·(θ+1θ)·C(T*),for everyθ>0. 1.1.2. Joint Replenishment Problem. With these preliminaries in place, an instructive way of viewing the joint replenishment problem is through the following question: How should we synchronize numerous EOQ models when different commodities are interacting via joint ordering costs? Specifically, we have at our possession a set ofncommodities, where each commodityi∈[n]is associated with its own EOQ model, parameterized by ordering and holding costsKiandHi, respectively. Here, a time intervalTibetween successive orders of this commodity leads to marginal operating costs of the form However, as previously mentioned, further complexity comes in the form of a joint ordering cost,K0, incurred whenever an order is placed, regardless of its particular subset of commodities. Given these ingredients, a joint replenishment policy can be represented as a vectorT=(T1,…,Tn), specifying the ordering intervalTiof each commodityi∈[n]. For such policies, the first part of our objective function corresponds to the sum of marginal EOQ-based costs,∑i∈[n]Ci(Ti). The second part, which will be designated byJ(T), captures long-run average joint ordering costs. This term can be formally expressed as Consequently, our objective is to identify a joint replenishment policyT=(T1,…,Tn)that minimizes long-run average operating costs, represented by 1.1.3. Joint Ordering TermJ(T). We mention in passing that, by glancing atEquation (1), it may not be clear whylimΔ→∞N(T,Δ)Δnecessarily exists for any given policyT. To address this question, for any subsetN⊆[n], letMNbe the least common multiple of{Ti}i∈N, with the convention thatMN=∞when these time intervals do not have common multiples. InAppendix A.1, we prove the next claim, showing that the above-mentioned limit indeed exists, along with an explicit expression. That said, the latter consists of exponentially many terms, implying that merely computing the joint ordering cost of an arbitrarily structured policy may not be trivial. limΔ→∞N(T,Δ)Δ=∑N⊆[n](−1)|N|+1MN. 1.2. Known Results and Open Questions In what follows, we highlight cornerstone results regarding rigorous algorithmic methods for efficiently identifying provably good joint replenishment policies. As previously mentioned, further background on historical developments, including classical papers such as those ofZangwill (1966),Veinott (1969), andKao (1979), can be attained by consulting directly related surveys and books. 1.2.1. Power-of-2 Policies. To our knowledge, the seminal works ofRoundy (1985,1986),Jackson et al. (1985),Maxwell and Muckstadt (1985), andMuckstadt and Roundy (1987)were the first to obtain efficiently constructible and provably good performance guarantees for the joint replenishment problem. Since then, these findings have become some of the most renowned breakthroughs in inventory management, perhaps due to their applicability in a wide range of lot-sizing problems. An in-depth discussion of these developments is provided, for example, byMuckstadt and Roundy (1993). Moreover, for very elegant methods of deriving these results, we refer readers to the work ofTeo and Bertsimas (2001)in this context. In a nutshell, the above-mentioned papers propose various methods for rounding optimal solutions to convex relaxations, ending up with so-called power-of-2 policies. The latter fix a common base,T0, with each time intervalTibeing of the form2μi·T0, for some integerμi. Surprisingly, whenT0is specified in advance, this ingenious mechanism for synchronizing joint orders determines a corresponding power-of-2 policy whose long-run average cost is within factor9/8≈1.06of optimal. Even more surprisingly, whenT0can be optimized, the latter factor can be sharpened to12ln2≈1.02, constituting the best-known approximation guarantee for the joint replenishment problem to this day. Consequently, as stated in countless papers, books, conference talks, and course materials, the primary open questions that motivate our work can be briefly summarized as follows: Can we devise stronger mechanisms for synchronizing multiple EOQ models, possibly outperforming power-of-2 policies? Could these ideas be leveraged to attain improved approximation guarantees for the joint replenishment problem? 1.2.2. Intractability Results. Digging from the other end of the tunnel, exciting progress has been made in the last decade regarding the plausibility of efficiently computing optimal policies. This line of work was initiated bySchulz and Telha (2011), establishing intricate connections between this question and fundamental problems in number theory. Specifically, they proved that when all time intervals are required to be multiples of a prespecified common base, a polynomial time algorithm for the joint replenishment problem would imply an analogous result for integer factorization. Subsequently, by exploiting the extraordinary work ofZhang (2014)on bounded gaps between successive primes,Cohen-Hillel and Yedidsion (2018)proved that the fixed-base version is in fact strongly NP-hard. The latter result has been considerably streamlined bySchulz and Telha (2024), showing that NP-hardness arises even in the presence of only two commodities. Finally, for the joint replenishment problem with arbitrarily-structured periodic policies, which is precisely the topic of our work,Schulz and Telha (2024)have recently extended their original findings to derive its polynomial-relatability to integer factorization. The latter result was further lifted to a full-blown strong NP-hardness proof byTuisov and Yedidsion (2020). Given this state of affairs, yet another repeatedly occurring open question is whether the above-mentioned evidence for intractability is best-possible or not, or put differently: Is the joint replenishment problem APX-hard? Alternatively, could this setting admit an approximation scheme? 1.2.3. Earlier Attempts. Interestingly, over the years, a handful of authors have been successful at deriving approximation schemes for several different variants of the joint replenishment problem. These efforts seem to have been initiated byLu and Posner (1994), who designed a fully polynomial-time approximation scheme (FPTAS) within the class of integer-ratio policies. Nevertheless, as demonstrated byRoundy (1985,1986), there is still a multiplicative gap of12ln2≈1.02between the best such policy and an arbitrarily structured one, meaning that this approach has an inherent constant-factor loss in optimality. More recently, approximation schemes have also been derived for the joint replenishment problem in its discrete-time finite-horizon setting, initially for periodic policies (Schulz and Telha 2011) and subsequently for nonperiodic ones (Nonner and Sviridenko 2013,Segev 2014). That said, limiting attention to an evenly spaced finite horizon goes around dealing with most synchronization issues arising due to the joint ordering termJ(T), whose rather convoluted structure was discussed inSection 1.1. In essence, such settings leave us with the much simpler task of coordinating a finite number of prespecified integer-valued time periods, and unfortunately, we are unaware of any way to leverage these ideas, so that one attains any nontrivial approximation for the classical joint replenishment problem. 1.3. Main Results The primary contribution of this paper resides in developing a new algorithmic approach for addressing the continuous-time joint replenishment problem, termedΨ-pairwise alignment, through which we devise a purely combinatorial algorithm for efficiently approximating optimal policies within any degree of accuracy. As formally stated inTheorem 1,Ψ-pairwise alignment allows us to determine a replenishment policy whose long-run average cost is within factor1+ϵof optimal. Quite surprisingly, as far as running time is concerned, these ideas fall within the notion of an efficient polynomial-time approximation scheme (EPTAS), whereϵ-related terms are separated from input-related terms. For anyϵ∈(0,12),the classical joint replenishment problem can be approximated within factor1+ϵof optimal. The running time of our algorithm isO(2O˜(1/ϵ3)·nO(1)). 1.3.1. Comparison with Power-of-2 Policies. Circling back to the list of open questions inSection 1.2, our work constitutes the first quantitative improvement over power-of-2 policies, which have been state-of-the-art in terms of provable performance guarantees since the mid-1980s. In fact, we believe that the technical ideas behindΨ-pairwise alignment and their analysis could provide approximation schemes for a host of additional inventory management settings; some of these potential applications are discussed inSection 4. From a purely practical standpoint, power-of-2 policies still appear to be indispensable at present time in terms of simplicity and implementability. However, it is worth mentioning that we have not made any attempt to optimize the running time expression stated inTheorem 1. In fact, we preferred to arrive at the simplest and easy-to-understand presentation, leaving quite a bit of room to spare by straightforwardly implementing several algorithmic steps. That said,Section 4will briefly touch on possible avenues for more efficient implementations. 1.3.2. Computational Characterization of the Joint Replenishment Problem. Complementing previously mentioned intractability results due toSchulz and Telha (2024)andTuisov and Yedidsion (2020), our approximation scheme for the joint replenishment problem resolves the long-standing open question regarding the computational complexity of this setting. As previously mentioned, the work ofTuisov and Yedidsion (2020)classifies joint replenishment as being strongly NP-hard, thereby ruling out the potential existence of a FPTAS. Consequently, because of the inevitable exponential dependency on the accuracy levelϵ,Theorem 1provides the best possible form of such dependency, up to perhaps replacing2O˜(1/ϵ3)by lower-order exponential terms. 1.3.3. Outline. Moving forward,Section 2starts off the technical part of this paper by providing a detailed account of our algorithmic approach. Subsequently,Section 3is dedicated to analyzing our resulting policy, showing that its long-run average cost is within factor1+ϵof optimal. Along this presentation, several technical arguments and auxiliary claims will be deferred toAppendix A, mainly for ease of exposition.
article.2. Algorithmic Overview:Ψ-Pairwise Align: In what follows, we lay down the specifics of our algorithmic approach, leaving its performance analysis to be separately discussed in subsequent sections. For this purpose, inSection 2.1, we begin by identifying a parametric regime where the joint replenishment problem can easily be handled. This preliminary step enables us to bypass certain irregularities while treating the complementary regime, which captures most technical hurdles. Next,Sections 2.2and2.3introduce the overall intuition behindΨ-pairwise alignment and present the basic objects that will be manipulated along with their related terminology. With these ingredients in place,Sections 2.4and2.5are intended to describe how such alignment can be efficiently computed as well as to highlight some of its structural properties, turning out to be very useful for analytical purposes. Finally,Section 2.6explains how joint orders and commodity-specific ones will be placed with respect to a givenΨ-pairwise alignment, collectively forming our approximate replenishment policy. 2.1. Two Parametric Regimes Let us make use ofT*=(T1*,…,Tn*)to denote an optimal replenishment policy, that will be fixed from this point on, withTmin*=mini∈[n]Ti*being the minimal ordering interval of any commodity. We begin by computing an overestimateOPT˜for the optimal long-run average costF(T*), such thatF(T*)≤OPT˜≤2·F(T*). This estimate can be obtained in polynomial time, for example, by computing a12ln2-approximate power-of-2 policy, as explained inSection 1.2. LettingTPO2denote the latter policy, we haveF(T*)≤F(TPO2)≤12ln2·F(T*), meaning that it suffices to setOPT˜=F(TPO2). For convenience, we plug-in an approximation guarantee of two rather than12ln2≈1.02, noting that the specific constant does not play an important role. 2.1.1. Easy Regime:Tmin*≥nϵ·K0OPT˜. In what follows, we argue that, when the minimal ordering intervalTmin*is much larger thanK0OPT˜, a very simple replenishment policy is actually near-optimal. To this end, letT^=(T^1,…,T^n)be the policy where, for each commodityi∈[n], we pick its intervalT^ias an optimal solution to a standard single-commodity EOQ model (seeSection 1.1), in which the ordering and holding cost parameters are given byK0+KiandHi, respectively. Namely, we overload the joint ordering costK0into each commodity-specific order, meaning that the long-run average cost function in this case becomesCi+(Ti)=K0+KiTi+HiTi. Accordingly, we haveT^i=(K0+Ki)/HibyClaim 1. The next claim, whose proof is provided inAppendix A.2, shows that whenTmin*≥nϵ·K0OPT˜, this policy happens to be near-optimal. WhenTmin*≥nϵ·K0OPT˜,we have It is important to point out that, because the value ofTmin*is unknown from an algorithmic perspective, we have no way of telling whether the optimal policyT*falls within the easy regime or not. Consequently, the above-mentioned policyT^will be kept aside as a candidate solution that will compete against the policy we construct in subsequent sections for the difficult regime. Eventually, the better out of these two policies will be our final solution. Along these lines, yet another important remark is that we can go around an explicit evaluation of the long-run average costF(T^). Without additional arguments, this operation requires a direct calculation of the joint ordering costJ(T^)via the closed-form expression inLemma 1, which potentially involvesΩ(2n)distinct nonzero terms. However,Lemma 2shows thatF(T^)≤∑i∈[n]Ci+(T^i)≤(1+2ϵ)·F(T*), meaning that we can instead evaluate the straightforward term∑i∈[n]Ci+(T^i). 2.1.2. Difficult Regime:Tmin*<nϵ·K0OPT˜. We proceed by further observing thatTmin*≥K0OPT˜; this bound can easily be derived by noting that As such, in the difficult regime, we know thatTmin*resides within the interval[K0OPT˜,nϵ·K0OPT˜). This property allows us to assume that we have at our possession an under-estimateT˜minof the minimal ordering intervalTmin*, specifically, one that satisfies The latter assumption can be enforced by noting that there exists an integerμfor which(1−ϵ2)·Tmin*≤(1+ϵ2)μ≤Tmin*, and we can therefore setT˜min=(1+ϵ2)μ, had we known what the value ofμis. The important observation is that, becauseTmin*∈[K0OPT˜,nϵ·K0OPT˜), it follows thatμ∈[log1+ϵ2(K0OPT˜),log1+ϵ2(nϵ·K0OPT˜)]. In other words, we have just identified a known interval that containsμ, of lengthO(1ϵlog(nϵ)). Moving forward, we will separately employ our algorithm for all integer values in the latter interval, eventually returning the best policy across these separate executions. Clearly, the resulting policy is at least as good (cost-wise) as the policy we would have obtained with an a-priori knowledge ofμ, simply since it is one of the values tested. With this estimate in place,Sections 2.2–2.6describe how our algorithmic approach operates in the difficult regime, recapping the nuts-and-bolts of these ideas inAppendix B. 2.2. High-Level Plan The fundamental intuition behindΨ-pairwise alignment can be succinctly stated as follows: Our objective is to efficiently construct a small-sized set of representative pointsR^⊆R+, guaranteed to simultaneously beϵ-dense andϵ-assignable. To better understand the last few properties, it is instructive to keep in mind the following interpretation: Small size:By a small-sized set of representatives,R^, we mean that its cardinality is upper-bounded as a function of1ϵand nothing more. ϵ-Density:The setR^will be calledϵ-dense when, by placing joints order at all integer multiples of all representative points, we obtain an ordering densitylimΔ→∞N(R^,Δ)Δthat matches the analogous densitylimΔ→∞N(T*,Δ)Δwith respect to the optimal policyT*, up to a factor of1+ϵ. Of course, by Representation (1), this property translates toJ(R^)≤(1+ϵ)·J(T*), implying that our long-run joint ordering cost is near-optimal. ϵ-Assignability:We say thatR^isϵ-assignable when, for each commodityi∈[n], we can choose an integer multiple of some representative inR^to serve as the ordering intervalT^iof this commodity, such that its marginal operating costCi(T^i)is within factor1+ϵof the analogous costCi(Ti*)with respect toT*. It is worth mentioning that, at the moment, even the mere existence of representatives that concurrently satisfy properties 1–3 is unclear. For instance, 0-density and 0-assignability can clearly be ensured by settingR^={T1*,…,Tn*}. However, a scenario where we end up with|R^|=Ω(n)cannot be ruled out, meaning that regardless of its computational aspects, this construction simply fails to obtain a small-sized set. 2.3. Preliminaries: Segments, Activity, and Representatives 2.3.1. Interval Classification. As our first step toward an efficient construction of the form described inSection 2.2, let us say that an ordering intervalTi*is large whenTi*>1ϵ·T˜min. In the opposite case, Inequality (2) implies thatTi*∈[T˜min,1ϵ·T˜min], in which case this interval will be referred to as being small. We further partition[T˜min,1ϵ·T˜min]by powers of1+ϵinto the sequence of segmentsS1,…,SL, such that 2.3.2. Guessing Active Segments and Defining Representatives. We say that the segmentSℓis active with respect to the optimal policyT*when there is at least one commodityi∈[n]withTi*∈Sℓ. LetA*⊆[L]be the index set of active segments, which is clearly unknown from an algorithmic perspective. We begin by guessing the precise identity ofA*, or equivalently, whether each segment is active or not with respect toT*. For this purpose, the overall number of guesses to consider is2L=O(2O(1ϵlog1ϵ)). Next, for each active segmentSℓ, letRℓ*be an arbitrarily picked intervalTi*that belong to this segment. We refer toRℓ*as the representative ofSℓ, noting that the exact value ofRℓ*is clearly unknown and that this definition is meant to serve only for purposes of analysis. 2.3.3. Basic Properties. We proceed by listing two useful observations regarding the set of representativesR*={Rℓ*}ℓ∈A*. First,Observation 1informs us that, for everyΔ≥0, the number of joint orders in[0,Δ]with respect to the time intervalsR*is upper-bounded by the analogous quantity with respect to the optimal policyT*; this claim can be straightforwardly inferred by noting thatR*⊆{T1*,…,Tn*}. In addition,Observation 2implicitly states thatS1must be an active segment, meaning that its representativeR1*belongs toR*. To verify this claim, it suffices to note thatTmin*∈[T˜min,(1+ϵ)·T˜min)=S1by Inequality (2). N(R*,Δ)≤N(T*,Δ), for everyΔ≥0. R1*∈R*. 2.4.Ψ-Pairwise Alignment Moving forward, our goal would be to efficiently compute a small-sized,ϵ-dense, andϵ-assignable set of representativeR^by approximately mimicking how the optimal setR*is structured. Specifically, we wish to synchronize the least common multiples of certain subsets of representatives that will eventually be shown to determine the joint ordering cost functionJ(·)up to lower-order terms. 2.4.1. Alignment Guessing. For this purpose, we say that a pair of active segmentsSℓ1andSℓ2is aligned when their representativesRℓ1*andRℓ2*have common integer multiples, which is equivalent toRℓ1*Rℓ2*being a rational number. Furthermore, lettingMℓ1,ℓ2*be the least common multiple ofRℓ1*andRℓ2*, this pair of segments is calledΨ-aligned when the corresponding multiplesMℓ1,ℓ2*Rℓ1*andMℓ1,ℓ2*Rℓ2*both take values of at mostΨ, with the latter constant set toΨ=2L2·2Lϵ=O(2O(1ϵlog1ϵ)). In this case, we make use ofα{ℓ1,ℓ2},ℓ1andα{ℓ1,ℓ2},ℓ2to denote these two multiples, respectively. Moreover,PΨ*will stand for the collection ofΨ-aligned pairs. Given these definitions, our next algorithmic step consists of guessing the setPΨ*. Put differently, for each pair of active segmentsSℓ1andSℓ2, we guess whether they areΨ-aligned or not, amounting toO(2O(|A*|2))=O(2O(1ϵ2log21ϵ))options overall. For each such pair, we additionally guessα{ℓ1,ℓ2},ℓ1andα{ℓ1,ℓ2},ℓ2; here, the total number of guesses isO(ΨO(|A*|2))=O(2O(1ϵ3log31ϵ)). 2.4.2. Obtaining Approximate Representatives. With these ingredients in place, our method for synchronizing common multiples, at least with respect to certain subsets of representatives, utilizes the following linear feasibility problem: Here, for each active segmentSℓ, we introduce a decision variableRℓ, playing the role of its so-called approximate representative. Constraint (A) states that each representative is picked within its corresponding segment. As a side note, because the right endpoint ofSℓ=[(1+ϵ)ℓ−1·T˜min,(1+ϵ)ℓ·T˜min)induces a strict inequality, to define a valid linear program, we plug in the closed segmentS¯ℓ=[(1+ϵ)ℓ−1·T˜min,(1+ϵ)ℓ·T˜min]instead. Constraint (B) ensures that everyΨ-aligned pairSℓ1andSℓ2has its representativesRℓ1andRℓ2related through the same multiples that relate the optimal representativesRℓ1*andRℓ2*. It is worth mentioning that some pairs could be aligned but notΨ-aligned, in which case there are no guarantees on how their common multiples are synchronized. Moreover, Constraint (B) involves pairs and nothing more, implying that (LP) does not include direct guarantees for arbitrary subsets of three or more representatives. 2.4.3. Feasibility of (LP). Let us first observe that the linear program (LP) is indeed feasible because{Rℓ*}ℓ∈A*forms a feasible solution by construction. In order to solve this problem, one can supposedly employ any black-box polynomial-time procedure for linear optimization, noting that every extreme point solution can be specified via polynomially many bits. To verify this claim, it suffices to observe that every entry in the constraint matrix of (LP) is integer valued, bounded byΨ=O(2O(1ϵlog1ϵ)). Similarly, every entry in its right-hand-side vector is either zero or a rational number residing within[T˜min,1ϵ·T˜min]. That said, inSection 2.5, we propose a graph-based method to obtain carefully constructed feasible solutions to (LP), allowing us to enforce several structural properties that will be imperative to our subsequent analysis. 2.5. Structure of Feasible Solutions 2.5.1. Alignment GraphGΨ*and Separability by Component. Recalling thatPΨ*designates the collection ofΨ-aligned pairs of active segments, let us consider the undirected graphGΨ*=(A*,PΨ*). Here, the vertex set is comprised of the active segments, and each pair of such segments is connected by an edge when they areΨ-aligned. We make use ofCC(GΨ*)to denote the underlying collection of connected components in this graph. The important observation to take note of is that the linear formulation (LP) is separable by component because there are no constraints tying between representatives from two different components. For this reason, we can focus our attention on independently solving the single-component programs{(LPC)}C∈CC(GΨ*), where 2.5.2. (LPC) Is a Single-Variable Problem. Focusing on a single componentC, letσCbe an arbitrarily picked vertex inC, to which we refer as the source of this component. Roughly speaking, the upcoming claim states that, in any feasible solution, once the approximate representativeRσCis fixed, the values of all other representatives are uniquely determined in a solution-independent way. The finer details of this claim are formalized inLemma 3, whose proof is provided inAppendix A.3. Interestingly, our proof is constructive, showing how to compute the undermentioned coefficients inO((1ϵ)O(1))time. There exists a collection of coefficients{βℓ}ℓ∈Csatisfying the next two properties: In any feasible solution to(LPC), we haveRℓ=βℓ·RσCfor everyℓ∈C. For everyℓ∈C,we haveβℓ=βℓ+βℓ−,whereβℓ+andβℓ−are integers bounded byΨ|C|. 2.5.3. Feasibility Region of (LPC). An immediate consequence of this structural result is that, in order to obtain a feasible solution to each of the single-component programs{(LPC)}C∈CC(GΨ*), there is no need to employ general-purpose linear optimization tools. Instead, for each componentC, we should simply identify the approximate representativeRσCof its sourceσCwith respect to some feasible solution, exploiting the constructive proof ofLemma 3to uniquely determine all other representatives. Along these lines, because Constraints (A) and (B) prescribe a nonempty polyhedral set, we know that the range of possible values forRσCforms a closed subsegment, say[rσC−,rσC+]⊆S¯σC. It is not difficult to verify that the left endpointrσC−is precisely the smallest value ofRσCfor whichRℓ=βℓ·RσCresides withinS¯ℓ, for everyℓ∈C. Similarly, the right endpointrσC+is given by the largest value ofRσCthat satisfies this condition. Based on these observations, bothrσC−andrσC+can be computed inO((1ϵ)O(1))time. 2.5.4. Preventing Coincidental Alignments. Although any value in[rσC−,rσC+]can play the role ofRσCin terms of being a feasible solution to (LPC), this choice will be made in a very specific way. Intuitively, for every pair of segmentsSℓ1andSℓ2in different connected components, we wish to ensure that their corresponding representativesRℓ1andRℓ2are misaligned, in the sense that their joint multiples occur very infrequently in comparison with those ofmin{Rℓ1,Rℓ2}. We formalize the latter notion by asking thatα1·Rℓ1≠α2·Rℓ2for every pair of integersα1≤Ψandα2≤Ψ, noting that this property may be violated by arbitrary solutions to (LP). That said, in the next claim, we show that misalignment is indeed achievable. We can construct a feasible solution to(LP)in which every pair of representatives belonging to different components is misaligned. Our construction can be implemented inO(2O(1ϵlog1ϵ))time. To end up with a feasible solution, it suffices to ensure that, for every connected componentC∈CC(GΨ*), the approximate representativeRσCof its sourceσCresides within[rσC−,rσC+]. Toward making a specific choice in this interval, we say that componentCis tight whenrσC−=rσC+; otherwise, this component will be referred to as being loose. Starting with the tight case, for any such componentC, there is only one possible value for the representative of its source,RσC=rσC−=rσC+. In turn, this choice uniquely determines the value of every other representative inC, byLemma 3. Given these decisions, we argue that for every pair of segmentsSℓ1andSℓ2in different tight components, sayC1andC2, their representativesRℓ1andRℓ2must be misaligned. To verify this claim, recalling that the optimal representatives{Rℓ*}ℓ∈A*form a feasible solution to (LP), we necessarily haveRσC1=RσC1*andRσC2=RσC2*, implying thatRℓ1=Rℓ1*andRℓ2=Rℓ2*, again byLemma 3. However, becauseSℓ1andSℓ2belong to different components, we know that they are notΨ-aligned, meaning thatα1·Rℓ1*≠α2·Rℓ2*for every pair of integersα1≤Ψandα2≤Ψ, which is of course equivalent toα1·Rℓ1≠α2·Rℓ2. Moving on to consider the loose case, we iterate through these components one after the other, in arbitrarily order. When each such componentCis examined, the important observation is that, for every already-fixed componentC^, either tight or loose, there are onlyO(|C|·|C^|·Ψ2)values in[rσC−,rσC+]that would create an alignment between a pair of representatives: one inCand the other inC^. Indeed, each such value corresponds to solvingα1·Rℓ1=α2·Rℓ2for some pair of segmentsℓ1∈Candℓ2∈C^, and for some pair of integersα1≤Ψandα2≤Ψ. Consequently, there areO(|A*|2·Ψ2)easily recognizable values to avoid in this interval, and we can guarantee that alignments will not be created by picking any other option, noting thatrσC−<rσC+. □ 2.5.5. Approximate Representatives vs. Optimal Ones. Yet another important consequence of the preceding discussion is more analytical in nature, being crucial for a number of cost accounting arguments inSection 3. As previously mentioned, the optimal representatives{Rℓ*}ℓ∈A*form a feasible solution to (LP), meaning in turn that their restriction to each connected componentCis feasible with respect to (LPC). As such, for every feasible solution{Rℓ}ℓ∈A*and for every componentC, we know that bothRσC*andRσCreside within[rσC−,rσC+], implying that there is a solution-dependent coefficientγC,R∈[rσC−rσC+,rσC+rσC−]for whichRσC=γC,R·RσC*. However,Lemma 3(1) extends this property to the entire component, as formally stated inCorollary 1. Here, we further observe that, because[rσC−rσC+,rσC+rσC−]⊆S¯σCand because the endpoints of the latter segment differ by a multiplicative factor of1+ϵ, we know thatγC,R∈1±ϵ. For every feasible solution{Rℓ}ℓ∈A*to(LP)and for every componentC∈CC(GΨ*),there exists a coefficientγC,R∈1±ϵsuch thatRℓ=γC,R·Rℓ*for everyℓ∈C. 2.6. Final Policy We are now ready to lay down the specifics of our replenishment policy, which will be denoted byT^=(T^1,…,T^n). To this end, given a feasible solutionR^={R^ℓ}ℓ∈A*to the linear formulation (LP), constructed according toLemma 4, we proceed in two steps: Placing joint orders:Joint orders will be placed only at integer multiples of the approximate representatives{R^ℓ}ℓ∈A*. In other words, withMR^ℓ={0,R^ℓ,2R^ℓ,…}standing for the integer multiples ofR^ℓ, we decide in advance to open a joint order at every point in∪ℓ∈A*MR^ℓ, regardless of whether any given point will subsequently be utilized by some commodity or not. Placing commodity-specific orders:For each commodityi∈[n], we determine its ordering intervalT^ito be the one that minimizes its marginal EOQ-based costCi(·)out of the following options. •Small intervals:Any of the approximate representatives{R^ℓ}ℓ∈A*. •Single large interval:LettingTimax=max{1ϵ·T˜min,Ki/Hi}, the additional option is⌈Timax⌉(R^1), where⌈·⌉(R^1)is an operator that rounds its argument up to the nearest integer multiple ofR^1. It is important to emphasize that, although choosing one of the above-mentioned “small” intervals as the ordering intervalT^iclearly falls within our set of joint orders, this also happens to be the case for the “large” interval option. Indeed, byObservation 2, we know thatR^1∈R^, implying that ordering commodityiaccording to the interval⌈Timax⌉(R^1)falls on integer multiples ofR^1, where joint orders have already been placed.
article.3. Analysis: The upcoming contents will be dedicated to pinpointing the performance guarantee of our resulting policy, showing that its long-run average cost is within factor1+ϵof optimal. To this end, following the high-level outline ofSection 2.2, our analysis proceeds as follows: EstablishingO(ϵ)-density:Recalling that the replenishment policyT^places joint orders at integer multiples of the approximate representatives{R^ℓ}ℓ∈A*, we argue inSection 3.1that the latter set is5ϵ-dense. Namely, its ordering densitylimΔ→∞N(R^,Δ)Δwill be upper-bounded by(1+5ϵ)·limΔ→∞N(T*,Δ)Δ, whereT*stands for the optimal replenishment policy. By Representation (1), this property directly implies that our long-run joint ordering cost is near-optimal, in the sense thatJ(T^)≤(1+5ϵ)·J(T*). EstablishingO(ϵ)-assignability:Concurrently, we prove inSection 3.2that the set of representatives{R^ℓ}ℓ∈A*is in fact2ϵ-assignable, meaning that our choice of the ordering intervalsT^1,…,T^nguaranteesCi(T^i)≤(1+2ϵ)·Ci(Ti*), for every commodityi∈[n]. In other words, we show that the marginal operating cost of each commodity with respect to the approximate policyT^is within factor1+2ϵof the analogous quantity with respect to the optimal policyT*. Evaluating long-run average operating costs:Finally, because our algorithmic approach employs numerous guessing steps to ultimately identify the least expensive policy out of all possible outcomes, it is imperative to efficiently evaluate the long-run cost functionF(·)for each of the resulting policies. As explained prior to derivingLemma 1, we do not know how to accomplish this goal for arbitrarily structured policies. However,Section 3.3is devoted to showing that the unique structural properties of our particular policies can be leveraged to estimate their long-run cost within any degree of accuracy. 3.1. Cost Analysis: Joint Orders 3.1.1. Intent. LetR^={R^ℓ}ℓ∈A*be the collection of approximate representatives, constructed along the lines ofSection 2. In what follows, we relate the ordering density of this set to that of the optimal policyT*, specifically arguing that To this end, it is worth mentioning thatN(R*,Δ)≤N(T*,Δ)for everyΔ≥0, byObservation 1. Therefore, Inequality (3) can be inferred from the next claim, whose proof is presented in the remainder of this section. For everyΔ≥0,we have 3.1.2. Inclusion-Exclusion with (Un)Crossing Sets. We remind the reader thatN(R^,Δ)stands for the number of joint orders in[0,Δ]with respect to the ordering intervalsR^. LettingMR^ℓ,Δ={0,R^ℓ,2R^ℓ,…,⌊ΔR^ℓ⌋·R^ℓ}be the set of integer multiples ofR^ℓwithin[0,Δ], we clearly have Let us classify a subset of segmentsA⊆A*as uncrossing when it is contained in a single connected component of the alignment graphGΨ*. In the opposite case, whereAhas segments belonging to two or more components, we say thatAis crossing. These two families of sets will be respectively designated byX¯andX. With this terminology, by the inclusion-exclusion principle,N(R^,Δ)can be written as We proceed by separately upper-bounding the terms (I) and (II) appearing in this expression. 3.1.3. Bounding the Crossing Term (II). Starting with the easier part, we argue in the next claim that the crossing term (II) is actually negligible in comparison with the number of joints ordersN(R*,Δ)with respect to the optimal representativesR*. At least intuitively, this claim stems from the observation that crossing sets necessarily contain at least one misaligned pair of representatives, making their joint orders very infrequent in comparison with the ordering density of an optimal replenishment policy. We formalize this notion while establishing the next upper bound. (II)≤ϵ·N(R*,Δ)+2|A*|. Let us first focus on a single crossing setA∈X, with|∩ℓ∈AMR^ℓ,Δ|being its corresponding term within the overall summation (II). BecauseAis crossing, this set has a pair of segmentsSℓ1andSℓ2in different connected components ofGΨ*. As a result, byLemma 4, we know thatR^ℓ1andR^ℓ2either do not have common integer multiples or have their least common multiple being greater thanΨ·min{R^ℓ1,R^ℓ2}. Therefore, Here, the third inequality holds because bothR^ℓ1andR^ℓ2reside within the interval[T˜min,1ϵ·T˜min], due to being part of a feasible solution to (LP). The fourth inequality is obtained by noting that according to Inequality (2), we have in particular(1−ϵ2)·Tmin*≤T˜min, and thusN(R*,Δ)≥ΔTmin*≥Δ2T˜min. It is important to emphasize that, for precisely the same argument, Inequality (5) is valid with respect toR*, meaning that|∩ℓ∈AMRℓ*,Δ|≤2Ψ·N(R*,Δ)+1; we will make use of this property later on. Given these observations, the crossing term (II) can be bounded by noting that 3.1.4. Bounding the Uncrossing Term (I). We now shift our attention to the more difficult part, claiming that the uncrossing term (I) does not deviate much above the number of joints ordersN(R*,Δ). This analysis is precisely where most structural properties instilled byΨ-pairwise alignment will play an instrumental role, and it is therefore advisable to keep in mind the main bullet points ofSection 2.5. (I)≤(1+4ϵ)·N(R*,Δ)+4·|A*|2·2|A*|. Similarly to how the proof ofLemma 6starts off, let us initially consider a single uncrossing setA∈X¯, with|∩ℓ∈AMR^ℓ,Δ|being its corresponding term within the overall summation (I). Our intermediate objective is to relate this term to the analogous quantity|∩ℓ∈AMRℓ*,Δ|, defined with respect toR*. For this purpose, lettingC∈CC(GΨ*)be the connected component ofGΨ*that containsA, we know byCorollary 1that there exists a coefficientγC,R^∈1±ϵsuch thatR^ℓ=γC,R^·Rℓ*for everyℓ∈C. In the next claim, we exploit this connection to show that the latter coefficient determines the relation between|∩ℓ∈AMR^ℓ,Δ|and|∩ℓ∈AMRℓ*,Δ|up to constant factors. The proof of this result appears inAppendix A.4. |∩ℓ∈AMR^ℓ,Δ|∈1γC,R^·|∩ℓ∈AMRℓ*,Δ|±2. Consequently, the uncrossing term (I) can be bounded by observing that (III)≤(1+ϵ)·N(R*,Δ)+|A*|2·2|A*|. □ 3.1.5. Putting It All Together. We are now ready to finalize the proof ofLemma 5. To this end, by pluggingLemmas 6and7into Decomposition (4), it indeed follows that 3.2. Cost Analysis: Commodity-Specific Orders 3.2.1. Intent. LetT^=(T^1,…,T^n)be the replenishment policy constructed inSection 2. We proceed by arguing that, for every commodityi∈[n], its marginal operating cost with respect to the approximate policyT^matches the analogous quantity with respect to the optimal policyT*up to low-order terms. The precise nature of this relation is formalized inLemma 8, whose proof is presented in the remainder of this section. Our analysis will be divided to three parametric regimes, depending on whether the optimal intervalTi*is small or large and on howTi*andKi/Hiare related. Ci(T^i)≤(1+2ϵ)·Ci(Ti*),for every commodityi∈[n]. 3.2.2. Regime 1:Ti*Is Small. Letℓ∈[L]be the unique segment index for whichTi*∈Sℓ, meaning in particular that this segment is active. BecauseR^is a feasible solution to (LP), it follows that the decision variableR^ℓindeed exists within this linear program, and moreover,R^ℓ∈S¯ℓdue to Constraint (A). Therefore,R^ℓis one of the options considered for our ordering intervalT^i, as explained inSection 2.6. Moreover, because we pick the option that minimizes the marginal EOQ-based costCi(·)of this commodity, Here, the second inequality holds because bothTi*andR^ℓreside withinS¯ℓ, which is a segment whose endpoints differ by a factor of1+ϵ, implying thatTi*1+ϵ≤R^ℓ≤(1+ϵ)·Ti*. 3.2.3. Regime 2:Ti*Is Large andTi*≤Ki/Hi. By recalling how large ordering intervals were defined inSection 2.3, we must have1ϵ·T˜min<Ti*≤Ki/Hiin this case, implying thatTimax=max{1ϵ·T˜min,Ki/Hi}=Ki/Hi. Because⌈Timax⌉(R^1)is one of the options considered in setting our ordering intervalT^i, and because we pick the option that minimizes the marginal cost functionCi(·), it follows that Here, Inequality (7) holds because the functionCi(·)is strictly convex, with a unique minimum atKi/Hi, as stated in items 1 and 2 ofClaim 1. Therefore, this function is strictly increasing over[Ki/Hi,∞), and the desired inequality follows by noting that 3.2.4. Regime 3:Ti*Is Large andTi*>Ki/Hi. Once again, because⌈Timax⌉(R^1)is one of the options considered for our ordering intervalT^i, we infer that Here, the second inequality holds because, as explained when analyzing regime 2, the functionCi(·)is strictly increasing over[Ki/Hi,∞). In addition,Timax≥Ki/Hi, and the desired inequality follows by noting that 3.3. Evaluating Long-Run Average Costs A close examination of our algorithmic approach reveals that, due to a sequence of enumeration-based procedures required for guessing the minimal ordering intervalTmin*, the set of active segmentsA*, and theα{·,·},·-multiples, we have generated a collection ofO(2O(1ϵ3log31ϵ)·logn)candidate policies overall. Thus, it remains to efficiently evaluate the long-run costF(T^)=J(T^)+∑i∈[n]Ci(T^i)for each of these policies and identify the least expensive outcome. While calculating∑i∈[n]Ci(T^i)is straightforward, it appears as if we would run into an exponential-time computation when evaluating the joint ordering costJ(T^). Indeed, byLemma 1, the latter function can be expressed asJ(T^)=K0·∑N⊆[n](−1)|N|+1MT^,N, whereMT^,Nis the least common multiple of{T^i}i∈N, with the convention thatMT^,N=∞when these intervals do not have common multiples. However, as explained inSection 2.6, our policyT^places joint orders only at integer multiples of the approximate representativesR^={R^ℓ}ℓ∈A*. This feature allows us to establish the next estimate for the joint ordering cost, showing that it is essentially determined by common multiples of uncrossing sets. The proof of this result is deferred toAppendix A.6. J(R^)∈(1±2ϵ)·K0·∑A∈X¯(−1)|A|+1MR^,A. Evidently, the approximate expression we have just obtained for evaluatingJ(R^)involves onlyO(2|A*|)=O(2O(1ϵlog1ϵ))summands. Moreover, because each of the setsA⊆Cis uncrossing, its approximate representatives{R^ℓ}ℓ∈Ahave common integer multiples, as explained within the proof ofClaim 2, with the additional observation that(∏ℓ∈Aβℓ+)·R^σCdivides byR^ℓ=βℓ+βℓ−·R^σCfor everyℓ∈A. In turn, the least common multipleMR^,Amust be of the formβA+βA−·R^σC, where bothβA+andβA−are integers bounded byΨ|C|=O(2O(1ϵ2log21ϵ)). As such,MR^,Acan easily be computed by enumerating over all possible values ofβA+andβA−.
article.4. Concluding Remarks: We conclude this paper with a number of fundamental questions for future research, ranging from seemingly doable to highly nontrivial. These prospective directions take aim at devising more efficient implementations of the core ideas presented inSection 2, as well as at examining whether our approximation scheme can be migrated to nearby inventory management models. 4.1. Hidden Constants Within theO-Notation As stated inTheorem 1, the current form of our algorithmic approach leads to an overall running time ofO(2O˜(1/ϵ3)·nO(1)). As explained inSection 2.4, the first term in this expression is actuallyO(2O(1ϵ3log31ϵ)), making one wonder whether extremely large constants are hidden within. When performing exact calculations along the analysis, it is not difficult to arrive at a constant of at most four. Moreover, with a substantially more grainy analysis, this constant can be made as small as1+O(ϵ), although such refinements will probably detract from the main ideas of this work. 4.2. Improved Implementations? Even though an exponential dependency on the accuracy levelϵis inevitable, an interesting direction for future work is that of arriving at lower-order exponential terms. One promising idea along these lines begins by observing that the2O˜(1/ϵ3)-bottleneck resides only within the guessing procedure for theα{·,·},·-multiples inSection 2.4. Here, each such multiple can be as large asΨ,and we may be required to enumerate overΩ(|A*|2)pairs of segments, ending up withO(ΨO(|A*|2))=O(2O(1ϵ3log31ϵ))guesses overall. However, a close inspection of the proof ofLemma 3reveals that, because we are picking an arbitrary spanning tree within each connected component, every nontree edge is completely overlooked. For this reason, our guessing procedure can be alternatively implemented by the following: Enumerating all possible forests over the set of verticesA*, where by Cayley’s formula (Aigner and Ziegler 2018, pp. 235–240), there are only|A*|O(|A*|)forests to consider. Guessing theα{·,·},·-multiples for edges of this forest, of which there are onlyO(|A*|). Consequently, the total number of guesses becomesO((Ψ·|A*|)O(|A*|))=O(2O(1ϵ2log21ϵ)), immediately leading to anO(2O˜(1/ϵ2)·nO(1))-time implementation. Yet another avenue for potential improvement is related to the following question: How large should the alignment parameterΨbe, to ensure that we are still computing a(1+ϵ)-approximate policy? On the face of it, because of the use of inclusion-exclusion-like bounds inSection 3.1, pickingΨ=2Ω(1/ϵ)seems an absolute necessity. To bypass this bottleneck, it would be interesting to examine whether alternative analytical ideas could allow us to work withΨ=poly(1ϵ), leading to an overall running time of onlyO(2O˜(1/ϵ)·nO(1)). 4.3. Extensibility to Additional Models? Within the scope of joint replenishment, this paper has been successful at developingΨ-pairwise alignment, a new mechanism for synchronizing multiple EOQ models, and in turn, for efficiently approximating optimal policies within any degree of accuracy. AlthoughΨ-pairwise alignment turned out to outperform power-of-2 policies in this context, we still do not know whether our methodology can be leveraged to address nearby inventory management models of similar nature. This research direction will be left as an intriguing open question for future work. We believe that the first candidate in line is very likely to be the one-warehouse multiretailer problem (Roundy 1985,Muckstadt and Roundy 1987,Lu and Posner 1994,Levi et al. 2008,Gayon et al. 2017), primarily given its structural similarity to the classical joint replenishment model. Additional candidates could be multiproduct lot-sizing problems revolving around assembly and distribution systems, where power-of-2 polices have been instrumental. Avid readers could delve into the finer details of these settings by consulting the excellent survey ofMuckstadt and Roundy (1993)and by going through the elegant analysis ofTeo and Bertsimas (2001)for several such problems.

article.Appendix B. Detailed Algorithmic Descrip: In this section, we provide a complete description of our algorithmic approach, directed mainly toward practitioners who may be interested in an easy-to-implement variant. To this end, one fundamental difference between the procedures outlined below and those proposed inSection 2is that we resort to explicitly solving (LP) rather than going through the purely combinatorial construction. In addition, we make use of random perturbation to ensure that the representatives of different components are misaligned. Both of these alterations are motivated by simplicity of implementation, noting that they are more difficult to rigorously analyze. To avoid an overly lengthy discussion, we assume that the reader is already familiar with the basic definitions and notation introduced acrossSection 2. B.1. Overall Procedure Step 1: The estimatesOPT˜andT˜min. Compute a12ln2-approximate power-of-2 policyTPO2, using any of the algorithms mentioned inSection 1.2, and setOPT˜=F(TPO2). Next, enumerate over all choices of the formT˜min=(1+ϵ2)μ, whereμ∈log1+ϵ2(K0OPT˜),log1+ϵ2(nϵ·K0OPT˜). Step 2: The easy-regime policyT^E. SetT^iE=(K0+Ki)/Hifor every commodityi∈[n]. Step 3: The difficult-regime policyT^D. SeeAppendix B.2. Step 4: Policy selection.Return the least expensive policy out ofT^EandT^D. B.2. Difficult-Regime Construction Step 3a: Active segments,A*. Enumerate over all subsetsA*⊆[L], whereL=⌈log1+ϵ(1ϵ)⌉. Step 3b:Ψ-aligned pairs,PΨ*. Enumerate over all options of deciding whether each pair of active segments isΨ-aligned or not. Step 3c:α-multiples.For each pair(ℓ1,ℓ2)∈PΨ*, enumerate over all multiplesα{ℓ1,ℓ2},ℓ1,α{ℓ1,ℓ2},ℓ2∈[Ψ], whereΨ=2L2·2Lϵ. Step 3d: Approximate representatives,R^={R^ℓ}ℓ∈A*. Obtain the collection of representatives by solving the feasibility linear program ofSection 2.4, Step 3e: Preventing coincidental alignments.For each connected componentC∈CC(GΨ*), independently draw a uniform random variableξC∈[1−ϵ,1+ϵ], and scale all representatives{R^ℓ}ℓ∈CbyξC. Step 3f: The replenishment policy.For every commodityi∈[n], letTimax=max{1ϵ·T˜min,Ki/Hi}. Then,
